currency.name: Chinese Yuan (Renminbi)
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: ¥80 (about £9). Note: many places only take phone payments.

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is China on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia. China takes up most of east Asia 🌏

mapQuestions.question: How many people live in China?
mapQuestions.answer: About 1.4 billion. That is more than 1 in 6 people on earth.

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of China?
mapQuestions.answer: Beijing in the north. The biggest city is actually Shanghai on the east coast, with around 26 million people.

mapQuestions.question: What is the longest river in China?
mapQuestions.answer: The Yangtze, the third-longest river in the world (6,300km). It flows from the high Tibetan plateau all the way to the East China Sea at Shanghai.

mapQuestions.question: How long is the Great Wall of China?
mapQuestions.answer: About 21,000km when you add up all the sections. It is not one wall but many, built over 2,000 years by different emperors.

foods.name: Jiǎozi (dumplings)
foods.description: Half-moon parcels of dough stuffed with pork, prawn or vegetables, then boiled, steamed or pan-fried. Eaten by the dozen, especially at Chinese New Year. Dipped in black vinegar and chilli oil.

foods.name: Peking Duck
foods.description: Roast duck with crispy mahogany skin, served sliced with thin pancakes, spring onions, cucumber and a sweet bean sauce. You wrap it yourself like a tiny burrito. Beijing's most famous dish.

foods.name: Hot pot
foods.description: A bubbling pot of spicy broth in the middle of the table. You dunk raw meat, tofu, vegetables and noodles in to cook them yourself. Sichuan hot pot uses red oil so spicy it makes your mouth dance.

foods.name: Lāmiàn (hand-pulled noodles)
foods.description: A chef stretches a lump of dough and slaps it on the counter, then pulls and folds it dozens of times until it becomes a hundred strands of perfect noodles, all in about a minute. Served in beef broth.

foods.name: Mápó tofu
foods.description: Soft tofu in a fiery red sauce with minced pork, Sichuan peppercorns and chilli oil. The peppercorns make your tongue tingle and go numb. A famous Sichuan dish.

foods.name: Bāozi
foods.description: Soft fluffy steamed buns with a savoury filling inside, usually pork or vegetables. Sold from bamboo steamers stacked five-high on every street corner at breakfast.

scavengerItems.label: Someone paying for something with a QR code on their phone (China barely uses cash any more)
A red dragon or lion design on a wall, doorway or souvenir
A red paper lantern hanging outside a shop or restaurant
Someone carrying a glass jar of hot tea around all day, topping it up at hot-water dispensers
Older people doing tai chi in a park at sunrise
A bowl of steaming bāozi in a bamboo steamer
A scooter or bike piled with an unreasonable amount of stuff
A bamboo plant being eaten by a panda (in a zoo or sanctuary) or as decoration
Curved pagoda-style roofs with little dragons on the corners
Someone practising Chinese calligraphy with water on a pavement
A high-speed train station the size of a small airport
A sign in traditional Chinese characters (which can be 200+ years old in design)

geoMatches.description: The northern capital. The Forbidden City (the old imperial palace), Tiananmen Square, and a section of the Great Wall just outside the city.
The biggest city in China and one of the biggest in the world. Skyscrapers on one side of the river, beautiful old colonial-era buildings on the other.
An ancient capital in the centre of China. Home to the Terracotta Army, an underground army of 8,000 life-sized clay soldiers buried with the first emperor.
The capital of Sichuan in the west. Home of the giant panda research base, where you can watch real pandas eating bamboo for hours.
In Guangxi province in the south. Hundreds of jagged limestone karst hills rising out of rice paddies, with the Li River winding through. Looks like a Chinese painting.
The "Roof of the World", a high plateau in the south-west between 4,000 and 5,000m above sea level. Home of the Potala Palace and the spiritual centre of Tibetan Buddhism.

senses.smell: Sizzling garlic and ginger from a hundred woks, jasmine tea, incense in temples, the dry hot dust of the north, monsoon rain on hot southern streets…
senses.hear: Mahjong tiles clacking in alleyways, scooter horns, "Nǐ hǎo!", the rumble of high-speed trains, tai chi music in a park, Mandarin's rising and falling tones…
senses.taste: Numbing-spicy Sichuan peppercorns, salty soy, sweet hoisin sauce, hot crispy duck skin, the burn of fresh chilli, hot tea drunk all day from a glass jar…
senses.feel: The crush of a Beijing subway at rush hour, hot greasy noodles on a cold day, the dry desert air of Xi'an, soft warm mist around Yangshuo's karst hills…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why are giant pandas only found in China?
stories.body: Giant pandas live wild in just a few mountain forests in central China, mostly in Sichuan. They eat almost nothing but bamboo, around 14 kilos of it a day. Bamboo is so low in nutrition that a panda has to eat for 14 hours a day to get enough energy, and it sleeps for the other 10. Their thumbs are not real thumbs, just a clever wrist bone that grew to help them grip bamboo. They are technically meat-eaters but evolved to eat plants, which is why their digestion is rubbish. There are only around 1,800 left in the wild. The Chinese national breeding centre in Chengdu has helped grow the numbers, and you can watch them rolling around eating bamboo all day.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Pandas spend 14 hours a day just eating. What would you do if you had to do one thing for 14 hours every day?

stories.question: How was a 2,000-year-old army accidentally found by a farmer?
stories.body: In 1974, a farmer named Yang Zhifa was digging a well in his field near Xi'an. About 4 metres down, his spade hit something hard. It was the head of a clay warrior. Archaeologists arrived and found an enormous underground army: about 8,000 life-sized terracotta soldiers, with horses and chariots, every single one with a different face. They had been buried in 210 BC, more than 2,200 years ago, to protect the tomb of China's first emperor, Qin Shi Huang. The army stood in formation, silent, in three huge pits, for over 2,000 years. The farmer (now in his 80s) sometimes signs visitors' books at the museum.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If archaeologists 2,000 years from now dug up YOUR street, what is one thing they would find that would totally confuse them?

stories.question: Why does no one in China use cash any more?
stories.body: Walk into a noodle shop in Beijing, a tea seller in a village, or a 5-star restaurant in Shanghai, and they all want the same thing: scan this QR code. China skipped over credit cards entirely and went straight from cash to phone payments using two apps, WeChat Pay and Alipay. Around 90% of Chinese adults pay this way, for everything from a 1-yuan ice cream to a new car. Street buskers have QR codes taped to their guitar cases. Grandparents in their 80s scan their breakfast. Many shops no longer have cash registers at all. Foreign visitors can find this confusing at first because foreign credit cards often will not work.

stories.question: Why is the Great Wall actually many walls?
stories.body: Most people imagine the Great Wall as one wall. It is not. It is a long network of walls, built and rebuilt by many different Chinese emperors over more than 2,000 years to defend the empire against invaders from the north. Add them all up and the total length is around 21,000km, about the same as the distance from London to Sydney. The most famous parts (the ones in photos near Beijing) are from the Ming Dynasty, around 600 years ago. Building it was brutal: hundreds of thousands of workers died over the centuries, and many were buried in the wall itself. From space, contrary to popular myth, you cannot actually see the wall with the naked eye.

stories.question: What was the Cultural Revolution?
stories.body: From 1966 to 1976, China went through a strange and painful period under its leader Mao Zedong. Mao wanted to wipe out "old ideas" and turn China into a perfect socialist country, so he encouraged teenagers and young adults to form groups called the Red Guards. They smashed temples, burned books, destroyed art, and publicly shamed teachers, scientists, and anyone who seemed too educated or too "western". Schools closed for years. Millions were sent from cities to work in the countryside. Around a million people died in the violence. When Mao died in 1976, the Cultural Revolution slowly ended. Many Chinese people today are still careful about how they talk about that decade.
stories.thinkingQuestion: When a country goes through something painful like this, do you think it should be openly taught in schools, or kept quiet? Why?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in China?
How is paying for everything with a phone? Is it easier or weirder than at home?
What is one thing here that feels totally different from anywhere else?
Which Chinese place has been your favourite so far?
Have you tried saying anything in Mandarin? Whose attempt is best in our family?
China has changed enormously in the last 40 years. What do you think your own country will look most different in 40 years?

wordSearch.hint: Chinese foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Feral pigeon
animals.description: In every city plaza and around every Buddhist temple.

animals.name: Koi carp
animals.description: In ornamental ponds at temples, parks and traditional gardens.

animals.name: Chinese dragon
animals.description: Not a real animal — but every temple roof, palace gate and festival has one. Spot the 5-claw imperial dragons.

animals.name: Sika deer
animals.description: In northern China and Hokkaido-style parks. Sacred at some Buddhist sites.

animals.name: Giant panda
animals.description: In Sichuan reserves like Chengdu and Wolong. Famously eats bamboo all day.

animals.name: Red-crowned crane
animals.description: Sacred long-legged bird, symbol of longevity. In Heilongjiang wetlands.
